However, the underlying momentum tells a different story: headline_mom has been flat-to-negative in the last three prints (0.12, -0.11, -0.05), and headline_3m_annualized is actually negative (-0.14%). This means the big y/y jump was driven by base effects (weak prints rolling out of the comparison window) rather than fresh price pressure. Core PCE remains remarkably stable and below target, sitting at 1.19% y/y with a 3m annualized pace of only 0.83% — core_distance_from_target is -0.81pp, and core_yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (-0.04pp). Core is the better read on persistence, and it shows no acceleration at all.

Given the outgoing month-over-month print being replaced next release, and current mom_gap_vs_outgoing of -0.26pp suggesting deceleration in sequential terms, the y/y print is likely to keep climbing somewhat further next month only if base effects persist, but the flat-to-negative recent mom trend caps upside. However, core PCE tells a different story: it ticked up to 2.01% yoy, its highest in the sample, with core 3m annualized at 2.13% — modestly above target and accelerating (core_yoy_change_3m +0.11pp). Core is the better read on persistence, and it shows inflation stabilizing right at/near target rather than continuing to decelerate meaningfully. The headline decline looks partly base-effect/volatile-component driven given the choppy m/m series (0.21, 0.23, -0.07, 0.07), while core's slow grind higher suggests underlying price pressure hasn't fully abated. Given headline is already at cycle lows with core stable-to-firming, the easy disinflation from headline may be exhausting itself. I'll call this flat-to-slightly down for headline next month, since the very low base and core firmness limit further headline downside, but there's no clear reacceleration signal either.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print above 0.25% or core PCE yoy pushing above 2.10% would signal reacceleration; a further drop below 1.95% headline would confirm continued deceleration.

With such a low base from the -0.79% print rolling out of the yoy window next month, headline yoy is likely to rise again mechanically even if m/m moderates. Combined with the strong current momentum, I lean toward another increase in headline yoy at the next release.

Core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.18pp (rising, not falling), and core_3m_annualized at 5.65% is well above the 2% target and above the trailing yoy print, meaning underlying momentum is reaccelerating even as base effects mechanically keep pulling headline yoy down. Headline_3m_annualized at 1.90% looks disinflationary on its face, but that's whipsawed by two near-zero prints that don't fit the pattern of the latest 0.42% reading; the trend within that window is upward (-0.01, 0.06, 0.42). Given core is the better persistence read and it's ticking up while headline's recent momentum (0.42%) exceeds its 3-month average, I expect headline yoy next month to be flat to slightly up rather than continuing its decline, with favorable base effects offset by firmer sequential momentum.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print back below 0.1% alongside core PCE yoy falling back under 5.1% would confirm disinflation has resumed.

view.reasoning:
The previous down call was correct: headline CPI yoy fell from 7.76 to 7.12, continuing a clear disinflation trend from the 8.98 peak, and it's now at the 12-month low. Momentum confirms this — headline m/m came in at just 0.26%, below the 3-month average of 0.42% and well under the falsifier threshold of 0.8%. The 3-month annualized headline pace (5.09%) is decelerating relative to the year-ago run rate, and the yoy change over 3 months is -1.10pp, a solid deceleration signal. Base effects from last year's high prints are also working in favor of further yoy declines next month unless a large positive m/m surprise occurs.

The core PCE picture is more mixed: yoy ticked down slightly to 5.46 from 5.61, but it's within a tight band (5.11-5.61) over the past several months, and the 3-month annualized core pace (5.61%) remains elevated and above recent yoy change trend (core_yoy_change_3m +0.35pp), suggesting core persistence hasn't broken decisively. Given my mandate weights headline for the specific next-CPI-release call, and the momentum/level evidence there is unambiguously decelerating, I lean down again, though core's stickiness tempers full conviction. The gap between core and headline (-1.66pp) reflects energy/food effects on headline that likely persist short-term.
view.report:
The previous down call was correct: headline CPI yoy fell from 7.76 to 7.12, continuing a clear disinflation trend from the 8.98 peak, and it's now at the 12-month low. Momentum confirms this — headline m/m came in at just 0.26%, below the 3-month average of 0.42% and well under the falsifier threshold of 0.8%. The 3-month annualized headline pace (5.09%) is decelerating relative to the year-ago run rate, and the yoy change over 3 months is -1.10pp, a solid deceleration signal. Base effects from last year's high prints are also working in favor of further yoy declines next month unless a large positive m/m surprise occurs.

The core PCE picture is more mixed: yoy ticked down slightly to 5.46 from 5.61, but it's within a tight band (5.11-5.61) over the past several months, and the 3-month annualized core pace (5.61%) remains elevated and above recent yoy change trend (core_yoy_change_3m +0.35pp), suggesting core persistence hasn't broken decisively. Given my mandate weights headline for the specific next-CPI-release call, and the momentum/level evidence there is unambiguously decelerating, I lean down again, though core's stickiness tempers full conviction. The gap between core and headline (-1.66pp) reflects energy/food effects on headline that likely persist short-term.

Recent monthly prints (0.31/0.41/0.44) are running above the trailing 3m average of 0.39%, and above the prior outgoing 0.34%, so momentum is still accelerating, not just elevated. Headline is now at the top of its recent 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.22 from the low, but the trend of the last three months points upward within that range).

Core PCE tells a different story: still declining, now 3.06% y/y, continuing a steady grind down from 4.86% thirteen months ago, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.23pp. This is the persistence measure and it says disinflation is intact. But my mandate is to call headline CPI specifically, and headline's own momentum (accelerating m/m prints, positive 3m annualized gap over y/y) is the dominant signal for the next print. The divergence itself (core falling while headline rises) suggests food/energy or base effects are pushing headline up temporarily, but three consecutive accelerating months is enough to lean up again, not flat.

I'm weighting the headline momentum here because that's literally what's being forecast, while noting core's disinflation as a moderating force limiting conviction.

Core PCE also ticked up to 2.91% from 2.86%, and its 3m annualized pace (2.97%) is consistent with continued gradual firming rather than reversal, even though core remains the better read on persistence and its distance from target (0.91pp) is little changed over the past year — a slow grind, not a sharp re-acceleration. The headline/core gap is small (-0.11pp), so food/energy are not distorting the picture much right now. Momentum in three-month annualized headline terms exceeding the y/y rate, plus core's own uptick, both point to continued mild upward drift into the next print rather than stabilization.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print below ~0.15%, or core PCE y/y dropping back below 2.8%, would signal the uptrend is stalling rather than continuing.

missing_inputs.why: Rising or anchored expectations would confirm whether the current uptick is likely to persist or fade.

I don't see enough deceleration signal to call this down, but the move from 2.99 to 3.00 is tiny, so this is a lean rather than a strong call.
